nodes.context: Italian American Mafia crime family based in Cleveland, Ohio, involved in racketeering, gambling, loansharking, and labor racketeering.
Labor union that the Cleveland family exerted influence over, profiting from labor racketeering and skimming Las Vegas casino revenue.
Italian American gang formed in Cleveland's Little Italy around 1913, focused on bootlegging, later became the core of the Cleveland crime family.
Sicilian immigrant brothers who formed a faction that broke away from the Lonardos and competed for control of the Cleveland underworld.
Sicilian immigrant brothers who initially led the Cleveland crime family; Joseph Lonardo was the first boss.
Network of powerful criminals including Lucky Luciano and Meyer Lansky; Frank Milano joined in 1931.
Mafia's governing body; Frank Milano became a charter member by 1932.
Chicago-based Mafia family; Cleveland family developed ties with it under Scalish.
New York Mafia family; Cleveland family developed ties with it under Scalish.
Mafia family based in Pittsburgh; Cleveland family had an arrangement sharing profits from Youngstown rackets.
Midwestern Mafia family; cooperated with Cleveland family in influencing Teamsters pension fund and Las Vegas casinos.
Midwestern Mafia family; cooperated with Cleveland family in influencing Teamsters pension fund and Las Vegas casinos.
Irish gang led by Danny Greene that competed with the Cleveland family for rackets in the 1970s.
Pennsylvania-based Mafia family listed as an ally in the infobox.
New Jersey Mafia family listed as an ally in the infobox.
Michigan-based Mafia family listed as an ally in the infobox.
New York Mafia family listed as an ally in the infobox.
California Mafia family listed as an ally in the infobox.
Louisiana Mafia family listed as an ally in the infobox.
Outlaw motorcycle gang listed as an ally in the infobox.
Gang listed as a rival in the infobox.
Italian American Mafia crime family based in Cleveland, Ohio, active since 1900s.
Mafia family based in New York, with a point man in South Florida.
Mafia family based in New York, with a Rochester faction.
Mafia organization based in Chicago.
Mafia organization based in Detroit.
Mafia family based in New Jersey.
Mafia family based in Los Angeles.

edges.evidence_quote: the Cleveland family exerted influence over the International Brotherhood of Teamsters (IBT), profiting from labor racketeering and the skimming of revenue from Las Vegas casinos
edges.context: Cleveland family exerted influence over the IBT for labor racketeering and casino skimming.

edges.evidence_quote: Frank Milano and the Mayfield Road Mob gradually replaced the Porrello brothers as the Cleveland area's premier Mafia group, with Milano becoming the official boss of the Cleveland family.
edges.context: The Mayfield Road Mob evolved into the Cleveland crime family under Frank Milano.

edges.evidence_quote: Joseph Porrello was declared the boss and recognized nationwide as head of the Cleveland family.
edges.context: The Porrello brothers took over the Cleveland family after the Lonardos.

edges.evidence_quote: Joseph 'Big Joe' Lonardo became the first boss of the Cleveland crime family.
edges.context: Joseph Lonardo founded and led the Cleveland crime family.

edges.evidence_quote: Milano joined the National Crime Syndicate, a network of powerful criminals which included Charles 'Lucky' Luciano and Meyer Lansky.
edges.context: Cleveland boss Frank Milano joined the National Crime Syndicate.

edges.evidence_quote: Milano had become one of the top Mafia bosses in the country and a charter member of The Commission, the Mafia's governing body.
edges.context: Cleveland boss Frank Milano became a charter member of The Commission.

edges.evidence_quote: the Cleveland family developed ties with important crime figures such as ... Tony Accardo, as well as the Chicago Outfit
edges.context: Cleveland family developed ties with the Chicago Outfit under Scalish.

edges.evidence_quote: the Cleveland family developed ties with ... the Genovese crime family in New York.
edges.context: Cleveland family developed ties with the Genovese crime family under Scalish.

edges.evidence_quote: an arrangement with the Pittsburgh crime family which entitled the Cleveland family to twenty-five percent of the Pittsburgh family's profits from rackets in Youngstown
edges.context: Cleveland family had a profit-sharing arrangement with the Pittsburgh family over Youngstown rackets.

edges.evidence_quote: The Cleveland, Kansas City and Milwaukee crime families also exerted influence over the multibillion-dollar Central States Pension Fund of the International Brotherhood of Teamsters (IBT)
edges.context: Cleveland, Kansas City, and Milwaukee families cooperated to influence the Teamsters pension fund.

edges.evidence_quote: The Cleveland, Kansas City and Milwaukee crime families also exerted influence over the multibillion-dollar Central States Pension Fund of the International Brotherhood of Teamsters (IBT)
edges.context: Cleveland, Kansas City, and Milwaukee families cooperated to influence the Teamsters pension fund.

edges.evidence_quote: a violent mob war between the Cleveland family and Greene's gang
edges.context: Cleveland family fought a violent war with Danny Greene's gang over control of rackets.

edges.context: Listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: DeCavalcante crime family
edges.context: Listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.context: Listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.context: Listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.context: Listed as an ally in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Celtic Club
edges.context: Listed as a rival in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: the Porrellos broke away from the Lonardos and formed their own faction
edges.context: Porrellos split from the Lonardos, leading to conflict.

edges.evidence_quote: Vincent 'Two-Gun Jimmy' Prato, the local caporegime from the Pittsburgh family, attempted to establish a monopoly over gambling and extortion rackets in Youngstown, a territory which had historically been shared between the two families. This resulted in a mob war
edges.context: Pittsburgh family fought a mob war with Cleveland family over Youngstown rackets.

edges.evidence_quote: John "Peanuts" Tronolone, a long-time Miami Beach resident and South Florida point man for the Genovese family, was named the new boss of the Cleveland family.
edges.context: A Genovese family member became boss of the Cleveland family, indicating a structural tie.

edges.evidence_quote: Anthony P. Delmonti, an associate of the Cleveland family and the Rochester, New York faction of the Bonanno crime family, became a confidential informant.
edges.context: An associate was linked to both the Cleveland family and the Bonanno family's Rochester faction.

edges.evidence_quote: Iocobacci inducted new members into the family, forged ties with the Chicago Outfit, the Detroit Partnership and the DeCavalcante crime family of New Jersey.
edges.context: The Cleveland family forged ties with the Chicago Outfit.

edges.evidence_quote: Iocobacci inducted new members into the family, forged ties with the Chicago Outfit, the Detroit Partnership and the DeCavalcante crime family of New Jersey.
edges.context: The Cleveland family forged ties with the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: Iocobacci inducted new members into the family, forged ties with the Chicago Outfit, the Detroit Partnership and the DeCavalcante crime family of New Jersey.
edges.context: The Cleveland family forged ties with the DeCavalcante crime family.

edges.evidence_quote: Ray Ferritto (a notorious hitman who worked for both the Cleveland crime family and the Los Angeles crime family).
edges.context: A hitman worked for both the Cleveland and Los Angeles crime families.
